ending: The book ends with the moment the whole series has pointed toward: Bebbanburg is Uhtred's. Slipping away from Aethelhelm's fleet at Grimesbi as Norsemen allied with the Scots burned it, Uhtred disguised his own ships as the supply ships the fortress was expecting, and the guards themselves opened Bebbanburg's Sea Gate to him. In the fighting that followed, the prince Aethelstan - stowed away with the help of Uhtred's son - killed Aethelhelm's champion, and Uhtred's cousin, refusing to meet him man to man, was cut down. Aethelhelm's scheme to bind the fortress to his family died with the defence, and the ealdorman himself did not survive its fall. Constantin's Scots, still in the field around the fortress they had come to take, found it held against them by its rightful lord and no longer worth the price; the siege that was meant to starve out a usurper ends with the Scots coming to terms and withdrawing. After a lifetime of fighting for other people's kingdoms, Uhtred of Bebbanburg holds his own: lord of the fortress at last, with his son as its heir - and with Aethelhelm's faction in Wessex now owing him a hatred that will outlive the old ealdorman.
in_short: With a fragile truce holding between Wessex, Mercia, and Sigtryggr's Norse Northumbria, Uhtred at last turns to his life's ambition: retaking Bebbanburg from his cousin. First he must stop a war - Ealdorman Aethelhelm has been scheming to break the truce and to have the prince Aethelstan killed under Danish colors, and Uhtred exposes the plot before the kings. Then the board shifts: Constantin of Scotland invades the Bebbanburg lands and besieges the fortress, and the trapped cousin hires Einar the White's Norse crews and looks to Aethelhelm for rescue. Uhtred threads between Scots, Norsemen, and West Saxons - using the mad bishop-pirate Ieremias, scouting Aethelhelm's fleet at Grimesbi and escaping when it burns - and finally disguises his ships as the fortress's expected supply ships. The guards open the Sea Gate themselves; Aethelstan, stowed away, kills Aethelhelm's champion; the cousin refuses single combat and dies; Aethelhelm does not survive the fall. The Scots come to terms, and Uhtred is lord of Bebbanburg at last.

recaps.text: Earlier in the series: Uhtred of Bebbanburg, a Northumbrian noble raised by Danes and still a pagan, was robbed of Bebbanburg, his family's great sea fortress, by his uncle when he was a boy; his cousin holds it now. He became the greatest warlord of the Saxon kingdoms, serving Alfred and then his children: King Edward of Wessex, and Aethelflaed, the Lady of the Mercians, whom Uhtred put on Mercia's throne. He destroyed the Danish army of Cnut Ranulfson at Teotanheale, crushed the Norse invasion of Ragnall Ivarson, and saw his old enemy Brida killed by his own daughter Stiorra - who is now queen in the north, wife of the Norse king Sigtryggr of Eoferwic, Uhtred's son-in-law, whose throne exists on a sworn promise never to attack Mercia. Uhtred raised and protects Aethelstan, King Edward's eldest son, whose claim to the succession is contested by the faction of Ealdorman Aethelhelm, grandfather of the rival prince Aelfweard. One attempt on Bebbanburg, years ago, already failed.

An uneasy truce holds between the Saxon kingdoms and Sigtryggr's Norse Northumbria, and Uhtred - based at Dunholm in his son-in-law's kingdom - wants nothing more than to spend it taking Bebbanburg back from his cousin. But the truce itself is under quiet attack. West Saxon troops have planted themselves in a fort on Northumbrian soil, churchmen are preaching holy war, and behind the provocations Uhtred begins to trace the hand of Ealdorman Aethelhelm, the most powerful man in Wessex, who profits from a war however it ends. Sigtryggr looks to his father-in-law to keep the peace his throne depends on, while Uhtred's own plans for his fortress must wait on the politics he despises. Along the way Uhtred deals with one of the stranger powers of the Northumbrian coast: Ieremias, the mad Danish shipmaster who calls himself a bishop and rules his own flock at the mouth of the Tyne, a man whose madness hides a sharp instinct for profit and information.

Uhtred unpicks Aethelhelm's scheme: the provocations on the border were bait for a manufactured atrocity - West Saxons killed under Danish colors so that outrage would force Edward into war with Northumbria, with the prince Aethelstan conveniently among the dead and the blame on Sigtryggr. Uhtred foils the ambush and then confronts the plot openly before the assembled powers, exposing the scheme where Edward, Aethelflaed, and Sigtryggr can all hear it; the truce holds and Aethelhelm's design collapses, though the ealdorman himself remains untouchable. Then the north upends Uhtred's plans entirely: Constantin of Scotland marches an army into the Bebbanburg lands and lays siege to the fortress itself. Uhtred's cousin, penned behind his walls, hires the Norse sea-warlord Einar the White and looks south for allies - and Aethelhelm, seeing a chance to gain a foothold and a marriage bond with the fortress, prepares a fleet. Bebbanburg, which Uhtred meant to pry from one enemy, is now the prize in a race between Scots, Norse hirelings, West Saxon gold, and Uhtred's own small band. His path takes him to Grimesbi among Aethelhelm's gathering ships, where he scouts the enemy fleet and slips away undetected when Norsemen allied with the Scots burn it in the night.

Uhtred takes Bebbanburg. With the Scots blockading the land approaches and the garrison waiting on Aethelhelm's help, Uhtred plays the one card no wall can stop: expectation. His ships, disguised as the supply ships the fortress is desperate for, are welcomed in, and the guards open the Sea Gate to their own destruction. In the savage fight inside the walls, the prince Aethelstan - who stowed away with the connivance of Uhtred's son - kills Aethelhelm's champion, and Uhtred's cousin, offered single combat and refusing it, is killed. Aethelhelm's hopes of binding the fortress to his family die in the same fight, and the old ealdorman does not survive Bebbanburg's fall - a death his son will lay at Uhtred's feet for years to come. Constantin's Scots, who came to starve out a usurper, find the fortress held by its rightful lord and no cheap victory left; they come to terms and withdraw. The book ends with the series' oldest promise kept: Uhtred, who was robbed of Bebbanburg as a boy and fought a lifetime for other men's thrones, stands lord of his own fortress, his son beside him as its heir.
